title: Multi-field Return Point Memory
authors: Nathaniel Croce, Hossein Salahshoor, D. Zeb Rocklin
abstract: Non-equilibrium systems display memory, a dependence not merely on their present environment but on previously applied fields. Multistable systems such as spin glasses, martensites and granular matter have exponentially many microstates consistent with an applied field, making their rich dynamics difficult to control. Control and order can be achieved through the concept of partial ordering, which we here generalize to systems subject to multiple control fields. We demonstrate, within the model system of the zero-temperature Ising model, that this leads to return-point memory, in which an applied sequence of fields restores the hysteretic system not only to a previous magnetization, but to a previous exact microstate. The multiplicity of fields grants more precise and complex control of the system, with different classes of operations displaying commutative and noncommutative behavior. This grants new insight into how physical systems can remember, learn, and be trained.
summary: This paper extends the concept of return-point memory from single-field to multi-field driving in disordered systems. It shows that while the order of applying different fields can lead to non-commutative behavior, the system can still be precisely controlled to return to exact previous microstates. This provides new insights into how complex, multistable physical systems can be trained or programmed.
main_problem: Investigating whether return-point memory (RPM)—the ability of a system to return to a previous exact microstate—persists when a system is driven by multiple independent control fields rather than a single scalar field.
main_result: The authors prove that multi-field driving leads to return-point memory where bounded field excursions restore the system to a previous microstate, while also demonstrating that non-monotonic field trajectories introduce non-commutativity.
method: The study uses numerical simulations of a zero-temperature Ising model and mathematical frameworks involving the 'no-passing' property and partial ordering of microstates.
model_or_system: A multi-field Ising model (MFIM) which generalizes the zero-temperature random-field Ising model (RFIM), featuring ferromagnetic coupling and multiple time-dependent control fields applied to different subsets of sites (e.g., checkerboard patterns).
key_observables: Magnetization (specifically even and odd site magnetizations), microscopic spin configurations, and the degree of partial ordering between states.
important_parameters: Coupling constant J, disorder strength sigma, field trajectories (monotonic vs. non-monotonic), and the spatial pattern of field application.
main_assumptions: The system is studied in the zero-temperature limit, meaning no thermal fluctuations are present and spins only flip to minimize energy.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows spin configurations and checkerboard field application; Figure 3 demonstrates the restoration of microstates in two-field and large-scale 10,000-field systems; Figure 4 illustrates spiral trajectories in field space and the resulting periodic returns to specific magnetization states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of multi-field control, defines the multi-field Ising model Hamiltonian, analyzes monotonic vs. non-monotonic field paths, proves the existence of multi-field RPM, and explores complex spiral dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Application and behavior of system with two external fields. (a) An arbitrary snapshot of a 10×10 subset of a 100 × 100 spin configuration at time t = 112: light blue squares represent a positive spin at the site (+1) and light red squares represent a negative spin (-1). (b) The Heven (green) and Hodd (purple) fields are applied in a checkerboard pattern across the 10 × 10 lattice of spins. (c) Time evolution of the external fields Heven (dark green) and Hodd (dark purple) over time, and the corresponding magnetization at the even sites Meven (light green) and the odd sites Modd (light purple) when Heven and Hodd are applied to a 100 × 100 lattice of spins in the checkerboard...

FIG. 2. Commutativity and non-commutativity in a two field system. (a) Two systems begin from identical initial configurations (I) and evolve under distinct monotonic fields trajectories in the (Heven, Hodd) plane. Path 1 (pink) first increases Heven, and then increases Hodd, while Path 2 (yellow) reverses the order. Because both field components vary monotonically and reach the same final values, the re- sulting final configurations are identical (IV) as depicted in (b). The intermediate configurations (II,III) differ and ex- hibit no relation. (c) depicts a similar setup to (a) starting with two identical systems, but they now evolve under distinct non-monotonic field trajectories. Path 1...

FIG. 3. Demonstration of return point memory in two-field and multi-field system. (a) The external fields Heven (green) and Hodd (purple) fields evolve over time as shown. Both fields begin at large negative values and rapidly increase to pass through spin configuration I and reach spin configuration II. The fields then vary independently over time until reconverging to produce final configuration IV, which is identical to state II. Notice that Heven and Hodd never exceed the extrema reached during state I, consistent with the no-passing rule, and thus states II and III are less than I. (b) 100 × 100 external fields evolve over time (only 10 fields are depicted on the plot) and are applied...

FIG. 4. Repeated return-point memory effect via spi- ral control dynamics. (a) A 200 × 200 spin system evolves under a spiral trajectory plotted in the (Heven, Hodd) plane. (b) A resulting similar behaving spiral occurs as the system evolves when plotting the magnetization at the even sites and odd sites of the system in the (Meven, Modd) plane. (c) The behavior of Meven and Modd are each demonstrated individ- ually when plotted against Heven. The three purple points demonstrate instances of return point memory in the system between when the path first intersects a point during the in- ward spiral and when the point is intersected again during the final increase.
